core_frameworks.name: Elements-Interconnections-Purpose (EIP) System Definition
core_frameworks.description: A system must consist of exactly three components: elements (the visible, tangible parts), interconnections (the relationships and information flows holding elements together), and a function or purpose (the goal the system serves). The three interact, but purpose is typically the least obvious and most determinative of behavior.
components: Elements: visible, tangible, countable parts of the system
Interconnections: information flows and relationships linking elements
Function/Purpose: the goal or behavior the system produces — deduced from observation, not stated intent
core_frameworks.when_to_use: When encountering any complex situation and needing to determine whether it constitutes a system and where behavioral leverage lies
core_frameworks.source_quote: a system must consist of three kinds of things: elements, interconnections, and a function or purpose.

core_frameworks.name: Stock-and-Flow Dynamics Model
core_frameworks.description: Systems are understood through stocks (accumulated quantities measurable at any moment) and flows (rates of change that fill or drain stocks over time). The level of a stock at any moment is the memory of all historical flows, and behavior over time is determined by the relationship between inflows and outflows.
components: Stock: an accumulation (physical or informational) measurable at a given time
Inflow: a rate that increases the stock
Outflow: a rate that decreases the stock
Dynamic equilibrium: the state when inflows equal outflows
core_frameworks.when_to_use: When analyzing why a system resists change, moves slowly, or oscillates — stocks provide the memory and momentum that govern pace of change
core_frameworks.source_quote: A stock is the foundation of any system... A stock is the memory of the history of changing flows within the system.

core_frameworks.name: Balancing Feedback Loop (Goal-Seeking Structure)
core_frameworks.description: A feedback loop in which a stock's deviation from a goal triggers corrective action to restore the stock toward that goal. It is self-correcting and stability-seeking, opposing whatever direction of change is imposed on the system. It is both a source of stability and a source of resistance to change.
components: Goal: the desired state for the stock
Monitoring/signal: detection of discrepancy between actual and desired state
Corrective action: adjustment of inflow or outflow to close the discrepancy
core_frameworks.when_to_use: When a system appears to resist policy changes or return to prior conditions despite intervention — a balancing loop is maintaining the existing state
core_frameworks.source_quote: This kind of stabilizing, goal-seeking, regulating loop is called a balancing feedback loop... A balancing feedback loop opposes whatever direction of change is imposed on the system.

core_frameworks.name: Reinforcing Feedback Loop (Self-Multiplying Structure)
core_frameworks.description: A feedback loop in which a stock's growth (or decline) generates conditions that accelerate further growth (or decline) in the same direction. It produces exponential behavior — virtuous cycles or vicious circles — and will ultimately destroy itself unless constrained by a balancing loop.
components: Stock with capacity to reproduce or grow as a fraction of itself
Gain rate: the fraction by which the stock amplifies each cycle
Runaway direction: growth (virtuous circle) or collapse (vicious circle)
core_frameworks.when_to_use: When observing accelerating growth or accelerating decline — a reinforcing loop is driving the exponential trajectory
core_frameworks.source_quote: The second kind of feedback loop is amplifying, reinforcing, self-multiplying, snowballing—a vicious or virtuous circle that can cause healthy growth or runaway destruction. It is called a reinforcing feedback loop... It generates more input to a stock the more that is already there.

core_frameworks.name: [IMPLICIT: Structure-Before-Blame Diagnostic]
core_frameworks.description: Throughout the introduction, Meadows consistently redirects causal attribution from external actors or events to internal system structure. Every time a problem is mentioned, she asks what structural property of the system produces it — never who or what external force caused it. This functions as a two-step reframing protocol: (1) identify the behavior, (2) locate its structural source within the system rather than outside it.
components: Step 1 — Resist external attribution: resist naming a person, event, or outside force as the cause
Step 2 — Locate structural source: identify which stock, flow, or feedback loop within the system generates the behavior
core_frameworks.when_to_use: When a persistent, recurrent problem has resisted repeated interventions or policy fixes — the structural source has not yet been identified
core_frameworks.source_quote: The system, to a large extent, causes its own behavior! An outside event may unleash that behavior, but the same outside event applied to a different system is likely to produce a different result.

core_frameworks.name: [IMPLICIT: Purpose-Primacy Hierarchy]
core_frameworks.description: Meadows repeatedly ranks the three system components — elements, interconnections, purpose — in reverse order of their leverage on behavior. Elements are most visible but least determinative; purpose is least visible but most determinative. This hierarchy functions as a leverage-prioritization framework: when intervening in a system, look last at elements and first at purpose.
components: Lowest leverage: elements (most visible, most often changed, least impactful unless they change relationships or purpose)
Medium leverage: interconnections (information flows, rules, relationships)
Highest leverage: purpose/function (least visible, most determinative of all downstream behavior)
core_frameworks.when_to_use: When deciding where to intervene in a system — guides attention away from swapping out personnel or components and toward examining goals and information flows
core_frameworks.source_quote: The least obvious part of the system, its function or purpose, is often the most crucial determinant of the system's behavior... The elements, the parts of systems we are most likely to notice, are often (not always) least important in defining the unique characteristics of the system.

core_frameworks.name: [IMPLICIT: Inflow-Outflow Symmetry Principle]
core_frameworks.description: Meadows repeatedly demonstrates that the same change in stock level can be achieved by manipulating either inflows OR outflows, yet human cognition systematically neglects outflows. This functions as a corrective decision framework: before defaulting to increasing inputs to solve a stock-level problem, always evaluate whether reducing outflows achieves the same result more cheaply or efficiently.
components: Inflow lever: increasing the rate of input to raise stock level
Outflow lever: decreasing the rate of drain to raise stock level (systematically overlooked)
core_frameworks.when_to_use: Whenever the instinctive solution to a shortage is to add more inputs — prompt evaluation of outflow reduction as an equivalent or superior alternative
core_frameworks.source_quote: A stock can be increased by decreasing its outflow rate as well as by increasing its inflow rate. There's more than one way to fill a bathtub!... Everyone understands that you can prolong the life of an oil-based economy by discovering new oil deposits. It seems to be harder to understand that the same result can be achieved by burning less oil.

decision_heuristics.rule: WHEN observing a persistent behavior pattern over time → DO search for a feedback loop generating that consistent pattern BECAUSE if a behavior persists, there is likely a mechanism — a feedback loop — creating it
decision_heuristics.source_quote: if you see a behavior that persists over time, there is likely a mechanism creating that consistent behavior. That mechanism operates through a feedback loop.

decision_heuristics.rule: WHEN trying to determine a system's purpose → DO observe its behavior over time rather than reading its stated mission BECAUSE purposes are deduced from behavior, not from rhetoric or stated goals
decision_heuristics.source_quote: The best way to deduce the system's purpose is to watch for a while to see how the system behaves... Purposes are deduced from behavior, not from rhetoric or stated goals.

decision_heuristics.rule: WHEN a stock is large relative to its flows → DO expect slow response to any intervention BECAUSE stocks take time to change, acting as delays, lags, buffers, and sources of momentum
decision_heuristics.source_quote: A stock takes time to change, because flows take time to flow. That's a vital point, a key to understanding why systems behave as they do. Stocks usually change slowly.

decision_heuristics.rule: WHEN a reinforcing loop is dominant → DO use the doubling-time shortcut (70 ÷ growth rate %) to anticipate how quickly the system will reach critical scale BECAUSE exponential growth is routinely underestimated
decision_heuristics.source_quote: The time it takes for an exponentially growing stock to double in size, the 'doubling time,' equals approximately 70 divided by the growth rate (expressed as a percentage).

decision_heuristics.rule: WHEN a stock-maintaining balancing loop is failing to reach its target → DO check whether compensating flows (drain or fill processes) are being ignored in the goal-setting BECAUSE a balancing loop must set its goal high enough to compensate for simultaneous draining processes
decision_heuristics.source_quote: A stock-maintaining balancing feedback loop must have its goal set appropriately to compensate for draining or inflowing processes that affect that stock. Otherwise, the feedback process will fall short of or exceed the target for the stock.

decision_heuristics.rule: WHEN a feedback loop delivers information about a stock → DO recognize that this information can only affect future behavior, not correct the current state BECAUSE information delivered by a feedback loop can only affect future behavior; it can't deliver a signal fast enough to correct behavior that drove the current feedback
decision_heuristics.source_quote: The information delivered by a feedback loop—even nonphysical feedback—can only affect future behavior; it can't deliver a signal fast enough to correct behavior that drove the current feedback.

anti_patterns.mistake: Focusing only on inflows when trying to increase a stock, ignoring the equivalent power of reducing outflows
anti_patterns.why_wrong: The human mind systematically underweights outflows; this leads to expensive input-based solutions when cheaper outflow-reduction alternatives exist and produce identical results
anti_patterns.source_quote: The human mind seems to focus more easily on stocks than on flows. On top of that, when we do focus on flows, we tend to focus on inflows more easily than on outflows. Therefore, we sometimes miss seeing that we can fill a bathtub not only by increasing the inflow rate, but also by decreasing the outflow rate.

anti_patterns.mistake: Underestimating the momentum of large stocks and expecting rapid change from interventions
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Stocks change only as fast as their flows allow; large stocks with slow flows will resist even large interventions for extended periods, making impatience counterproductive
anti_patterns.source_quote: People often underestimate the inherent momentum of a stock. It takes a long time for populations to grow or stop growing, for wood to accumulate in a forest, for a reservoir to fill up, for a mine to be depleted.

anti_patterns.mistake: Attributing system behavior to external actors, events, or forces rather than to the system's internal structure
anti_patterns.why_wrong: The same external event applied to a different system produces different results; therefore the system's structure — not the event — is the true source of behavior, and blaming external causes prevents structural diagnosis
anti_patterns.source_quote: Political leaders don't cause recessions or economic booms. Ups and downs are inherent in the structure of the market economy... The system, to a large extent, causes its own behavior!

anti_patterns.mistake: Changing the elements (people, components) of a system while leaving interconnections and purpose intact, and expecting significant behavioral change
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Elements are the least impactful lever in a system; a system generally goes on being itself even with complete substitution of elements as long as interconnections and purposes remain intact
anti_patterns.source_quote: A system generally goes on being itself, changing only slowly if at all, even with complete substitutions of its elements—as long as its interconnections and purposes remain intact.

anti_patterns.mistake: Reading stated goals or missions to determine what a system is trying to achieve, rather than observing actual behavior
anti_patterns.why_wrong: A government that proclaims environmental protection but allocates little money toward it is not, in fact, pursuing environmental protection; only behavior reveals actual purpose
anti_patterns.source_quote: If a government proclaims its interest in protecting the environment but allocates little money or effort toward that goal, environmental protection is not, in fact, the government's purpose. Purposes are deduced from behavior, not from rhetoric or stated goals.

anti_patterns.mistake: Looking for a single external 'fix' (technical solution, new person, policy lever) for persistent systemic problems
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Problems rooted in internal system structure — hunger, poverty, drug addiction, war — will persist regardless of external fixes because the structural source continues to generate the behavior
anti_patterns.source_quote: Hunger, poverty, environmental degradation, economic instability, unemployment, chronic disease, drug addiction, and war, for example, persist in spite of the analytical ability and technical brilliance that have been directed toward eradicating them... That is because they are intrinsically systems problems—undesirable behaviors characteristic of the system structures that produce them.

trigger_questions.question: Am I looking at a system or just a bunch of stuff? Can I identify parts? Do the parts affect each other? Do the parts together produce an effect different from each part alone? Does this behavior persist across varying circumstances?
trigger_questions.purpose: Determines whether a situation warrants systems analysis; confirms the presence of genuine systemic interdependence before applying system tools
trigger_questions.source_quote: How to know whether you are looking at a system or just a bunch of stuff: A) Can you identify parts? . . . and B) Do the parts affect each other? . . . and C) Do the parts together produce an effect that is different from the effect of each part on its own? . . . and perhaps D) Does the effect, the behavior over time, persist in a variety of circumstances?

trigger_questions.question: If A causes B, is it possible that B also causes A?
trigger_questions.purpose: Reveals hidden feedback loops; shifts thinking from linear cause-effect chains to circular causality, which is the foundational move of systems analysis
trigger_questions.source_quote: If A causes B, is it possible that B also causes A?

trigger_questions.question: What is the actual behavior of this system over time — and what does that behavior (not the stated mission) reveal about its true purpose?
trigger_questions.purpose: Strips away rhetoric and stated goals to expose the purpose actually being served by the system's structure, which is the highest-leverage point for intervention
trigger_questions.source_quote: The best way to deduce the system's purpose is to watch for a while to see how the system behaves... Purposes are deduced from behavior, not from rhetoric or stated goals.

trigger_questions.question: Can I think of any human decision that is made without any feedback about the level of the stock it influences?
trigger_questions.purpose: Trains the practitioner to see feedback loops everywhere — used pedagogically to make the ubiquity of feedback visible and to reveal how thoroughly decisions are embedded in system structure
trigger_questions.source_quote: I often challenge my students to try to think of any human decision that occurs without a feedback loop—that is, a decision that is made without regard to any information about the level of the stock it influences.

trigger_questions.question: Instead of asking who is to blame, ask: What is the system?
trigger_questions.purpose: Redirects causal analysis from individual actors to structural sources of behavior; the foundational reframe that makes systems thinking actionable
trigger_questions.source_quote: You'll stop looking for who's to blame; instead you'll start asking, 'What's the system?' The concept of feedback opens up the idea that a system can cause its own behavior.

Interconnections: information flows and relationships linking elements

unified_frameworks.description: A two-step reframing protocol that consistently redirects causal attribution from external actors or events to internal system structure. (1) Identify the behavior pattern; (2) locate its structural source within the system rather than outside it. Meadows applies this throughout as the foundational epistemic move of systems thinking.
components: Step 1 — Resist external attribution: resist naming a person, event, or outside force as the cause
Step 2 — Locate structural source: identify which stock, flow, or feedback loop within the system generates the behavior
unified_frameworks.when_to_use: When a persistent, recurrent problem has resisted repeated interventions or policy fixes — the structural source has not yet been identified
unified_frameworks.source_quote: The system, to a large extent, causes its own behavior! An outside event may unleash that behavior, but the same outside event applied to a different system is likely to produce a different result.

unified_frameworks.name: [IMPLICIT] Purpose-Primacy Hierarchy
unified_frameworks.description: A leverage-prioritization framework that ranks the three EIP system components in reverse order of their impact on system behavior. Elements are most visible but least determinative; purpose is least visible but most determinative. When intervening in a system, look last at elements and first at purpose.
components: Lowest leverage: elements (most visible, most often changed, least impactful unless they alter relationships or purpose)
Medium leverage: interconnections (information flows, rules, relationships)
Highest leverage: purpose/function (least visible, most determinative of all downstream behavior)
unified_frameworks.when_to_use: When deciding where to intervene in a system — guides attention away from swapping out personnel or components and toward examining goals and information flows

The elements, the parts of systems we are most likely to notice, are often (not always) least important in defining the unique characteristics of the system.

unified_frameworks.name: [IMPLICIT] Inflow-Outflow Symmetry Principle
unified_frameworks.description: The same change in stock level can be achieved by manipulating either inflows OR outflows, yet human cognition systematically neglects outflows. Functions as a corrective decision framework: before defaulting to increasing inputs to solve a stock-level problem, always evaluate whether reducing outflows achieves the same result more cheaply or efficiently.
components: Inflow lever: increasing the rate of input to raise stock level
Outflow lever: decreasing the rate of drain to raise stock level (systematically overlooked)
unified_frameworks.when_to_use: Whenever the instinctive solution to a shortage is to add more inputs — prompt evaluation of outflow reduction as an equivalent or superior alternative
unified_frameworks.source_quote: A stock can be increased by decreasing its outflow rate as well as by increasing its inflow rate. There's more than one way to fill a bathtub!... Everyone understands that you can prolong the life of an oil-based economy by discovering new oil deposits. It seems to be harder to understand that the same result can be achieved by burning less oil.

unified_frameworks.name: [META] Feedback Loop Typology
unified_frameworks.description: An overarching classification of all feedback mechanisms into two fundamental types — balancing (goal-seeking, stabilizing) and reinforcing (self-amplifying, destabilizing) — from which all complex system behaviors ultimately derive. Real systems typically contain interlocking combinations of both types.
components: Balancing Feedback Loop: opposes change, seeks goal state, produces stability or resistance
Reinforcing Feedback Loop: amplifies change, accelerates growth or collapse, produces exponential trajectories
unified_frameworks.when_to_use: As the master classification lens when diagnosing any system behavior — determine which loop type is currently dominant
unified_frameworks.source_quote: if you see a behavior that persists over time, there is likely a mechanism creating that consistent behavior. That mechanism operates through a feedback loop.

unified_heuristics.rule: WHEN observing a persistent behavior pattern over time → DO search for a feedback loop generating that consistent pattern BECAUSE if a behavior persists, there is likely a mechanism — a feedback loop — creating it
unified_heuristics.source_quote: if you see a behavior that persists over time, there is likely a mechanism creating that consistent behavior. That mechanism operates through a feedback loop.

unified_heuristics.rule: WHEN trying to determine a system's purpose → DO observe its behavior over time rather than reading its stated mission BECAUSE purposes are deduced from behavior, not from rhetoric or stated goals
unified_heuristics.source_quote: The best way to deduce the system's purpose is to watch for a while to see how the system behaves... Purposes are deduced from behavior, not from rhetoric or stated goals.

unified_heuristics.rule: WHEN a stock is large relative to its flows → DO expect slow response to any intervention BECAUSE stocks take time to change, acting as delays, lags, buffers, and sources of momentum
unified_heuristics.source_quote: A stock takes time to change, because flows take time to flow. That's a vital point, a key to understanding why systems behave as they do. Stocks usually change slowly.

unified_heuristics.rule: WHEN a reinforcing loop is dominant → DO use the doubling-time shortcut (70 ÷ growth rate %) to anticipate how quickly the system will reach critical scale BECAUSE exponential growth is routinely underestimated
unified_heuristics.source_quote: The time it takes for an exponentially growing stock to double in size, the 'doubling time,' equals approximately 70 divided by the growth rate (expressed as a percentage).

unified_heuristics.rule: WHEN a stock-maintaining balancing loop is failing to reach its target → DO check whether compensating flows (drain or fill processes) are being ignored in the goal-setting BECAUSE a balancing loop must set its goal high enough to compensate for simultaneous draining processes
unified_heuristics.source_quote: A stock-maintaining balancing feedback loop must have its goal set appropriately to compensate for draining or inflowing processes that affect that stock. Otherwise, the feedback process will fall short of or exceed the target for the stock.

unified_heuristics.rule: WHEN a feedback loop delivers information about a stock → DO recognize that this information can only affect future behavior, not correct the current state BECAUSE information delivered by a feedback loop can only affect future behavior; it can't deliver a signal fast enough to correct behavior that drove the current feedback
unified_heuristics.source_quote: The information delivered by a feedback loop—even nonphysical feedback—can only affect future behavior; it can't deliver a signal fast enough to correct behavior that drove the current feedback.

unified_anti_patterns.mistake: Focusing only on inflows when trying to increase a stock, ignoring the equivalent power of reducing outflows
unified_anti_patterns.why_wrong: The human mind systematically underweights outflows; this leads to expensive input-based solutions when cheaper outflow-reduction alternatives exist and produce identical results
unified_anti_patterns.source_quote: The human mind seems to focus more easily on stocks than on flows. On top of that, when we do focus on flows, we tend to focus on inflows more easily than on outflows. Therefore, we sometimes miss seeing that we can fill a bathtub not only by increasing the inflow rate, but also by decreasing the outflow rate.
unified_anti_patterns.severity: High — leads to systematically suboptimal resource allocation decisions

unified_anti_patterns.mistake: Underestimating the momentum of large stocks and expecting rapid change from interventions
unified_anti_patterns.why_wrong: Stocks change only as fast as their flows allow; large stocks with slow flows will resist even large interventions for extended periods, making impatience counterproductive
unified_anti_patterns.source_quote: People often underestimate the inherent momentum of a stock. It takes a long time for populations to grow or stop growing, for wood to accumulate in a forest, for a reservoir to fill up, for a mine to be depleted.
unified_anti_patterns.severity: High — produces premature abandonment of correct interventions and thrashing between policies

unified_anti_patterns.mistake: Attributing system behavior to external actors, events, or forces rather than to the system's internal structure
unified_anti_patterns.why_wrong: The same external event applied to a different system produces different results; therefore the system's structure — not the event — is the true source of behavior, and blaming external causes prevents structural diagnosis
unified_anti_patterns.source_quote: Political leaders don't cause recessions or economic booms. Ups and downs are inherent in the structure of the market economy... The system, to a large extent, causes its own behavior!
unified_anti_patterns.severity: Critical — forecloses structural diagnosis entirely; no leverage can be found until this mistake is corrected

unified_anti_patterns.mistake: Changing the elements (people, components) of a system while leaving interconnections and purpose intact, and expecting significant behavioral change
unified_anti_patterns.why_wrong: Elements are the least impactful lever in a system; a system generally goes on being itself even with complete substitution of elements as long as interconnections and purposes remain intact
unified_anti_patterns.source_quote: A system generally goes on being itself, changing only slowly if at all, even with complete substitutions of its elements—as long as its interconnections and purposes remain intact.
unified_anti_patterns.severity: High — produces the illusion of reform while leaving the structural source of behavior untouched

unified_anti_patterns.mistake: Reading stated goals or missions to determine what a system is trying to achieve, rather than observing actual behavior
unified_anti_patterns.why_wrong: A government that proclaims environmental protection but allocates little money toward it is not, in fact, pursuing environmental protection; only behavior reveals actual purpose
unified_anti_patterns.source_quote: If a government proclaims its interest in protecting the environment but allocates little money or effort toward that goal, environmental protection is not, in fact, the government's purpose. Purposes are deduced from behavior, not from rhetoric or stated goals.
unified_anti_patterns.severity: High — misdirects analysis toward declared intent rather than structural reality

unified_anti_patterns.mistake: Looking for a single external 'fix' (technical solution, new person, policy lever) for persistent systemic problems
unified_anti_patterns.why_wrong: Problems rooted in internal system structure — hunger, poverty, drug addiction, war — will persist regardless of external fixes because the structural source continues to generate the behavior
unified_anti_patterns.source_quote: Hunger, poverty, environmental degradation, economic instability, unemployment, chronic disease, drug addiction, and war, for example, persist in spite of the analytical ability and technical brilliance that have been directed toward eradicating them... That is because they are intrinsically systems problems—undesirable behaviors characteristic of the system structures that produce them.
unified_anti_patterns.severity: Critical — produces repeated policy failure and wasted resources without addressing root cause

unified_trigger_questions.question: Am I looking at a system or just a bunch of stuff? Can I identify parts? Do the parts affect each other? Do the parts together produce an effect different from each part alone? Does this behavior persist across varying circumstances?
unified_trigger_questions.purpose: Determines whether a situation warrants systems analysis; confirms the presence of genuine systemic interdependence before applying system tools
unified_trigger_questions.source_quote: How to know whether you are looking at a system or just a bunch of stuff: A) Can you identify parts? . . . and B) Do the parts affect each other? . . . and C) Do the parts together produce an effect that is different from the effect of each part on its own? . . . and perhaps D) Does the effect, the behavior over time, persist in a variety of circumstances?
unified_trigger_questions.when_to_ask: Entry point — ask before applying any systems tool; gates the entire analytical process

unified_trigger_questions.question: If A causes B, is it possible that B also causes A?
unified_trigger_questions.purpose: Reveals hidden feedback loops; shifts thinking from linear cause-effect chains to circular causality, which is the foundational move of systems analysis
unified_trigger_questions.source_quote: If A causes B, is it possible that B also causes A?
unified_trigger_questions.when_to_ask: Whenever a linear causal narrative is offered for a complex phenomenon

unified_trigger_questions.question: What is the actual behavior of this system over time — and what does that behavior (not the stated mission) reveal about its true purpose?
unified_trigger_questions.purpose: Strips away rhetoric and stated goals to expose the purpose actually being served by the system's structure, which is the highest-leverage point for intervention
unified_trigger_questions.source_quote: The best way to deduce the system's purpose is to watch for a while to see how the system behaves... Purposes are deduced from behavior, not from rhetoric or stated goals.
unified_trigger_questions.when_to_ask: When diagnosing why a system persistently produces undesirable outcomes despite stated good intentions

unified_trigger_questions.question: Can I think of any human decision that is made without any feedback about the level of the stock it influences?
unified_trigger_questions.purpose: Trains the practitioner to see feedback loops everywhere — used pedagogically to make the ubiquity of feedback visible and to reveal how thoroughly decisions are embedded in system structure
unified_trigger_questions.source_quote: I often challenge my students to try to think of any human decision that occurs without a feedback loop—that is, a decision that is made without regard to any information about the level of the stock it influences.
unified_trigger_questions.when_to_ask: When introducing systems thinking to new practitioners or when a decision-maker believes they are acting independently of system feedback

unified_trigger_questions.question: Instead of asking who is to blame, ask: What is the system?
unified_trigger_questions.purpose: Redirects causal analysis from individual actors to structural sources of behavior; the foundational reframe that makes systems thinking actionable
unified_trigger_questions.source_quote: You'll stop looking for who's to blame; instead you'll start asking, 'What's the system?' The concept of feedback opens up the idea that a system can cause its own behavior.
unified_trigger_questions.when_to_ask: Any time blame, credit, or hero/villain narratives are being applied to a complex recurring problem
